source.title: Temporal binding within and across events

confidence_rationale: Within-event vs across-event encoding uses different hippocampal-PFC mechanisms: within-event = HC-vmPFC functional connectivity, across-event = univariate HC + lvlPFC activation. Validates that boundaries are computationally distinguished events for downstream consumers.
failure_signatures: If the substrate's segment_id transitions did not produce a distinct downstream signature (no flag indicating 'this transition crossed a boundary'), consumers could not differentiate within-event from across-event encoding -- collapsing biology's two functional regimes.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Successful serial encoding within events relies on increased HC-vmPFC functional connectivity; successful encoding across event boundaries relies on univariate activation in middle hippocampus and left ventrolateral PFC. The two regimes are functionally dissociable.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-288 segment_id transitions must be observable as discrete events to downstream consumers, not merely as continuous changes in context. The substrate API should expose a boundary_emitted flag at the moment of segment_id transition so consumers can switch processing modes.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The within/across distinction here is in human fMRI of episodic encoding; substrate consumers in REE (anchor sets, V_s readouts) operate at finer temporal scales and on simpler latent streams. The functional-distinction principle transfers; the specific neural implementation does not.
mapping.source_context: fMRI of healthy human adults; consecutive face and object stimuli encoding.

confidence_components.notes: Solid replication-class fMRI evidence. Mapping moderate because the architectural lesson (expose boundary as discrete signal) is what matters; the specific neural circuitry does not transfer.
